quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following sets of ordered pairs is a function? {(1,3), (2,5), (3,7), (4,9)}

options.text: Yes, but only if the y-values are all different
No, because the x-values are consecutive
No, because the y-values are all odd
Yes, it is a function because each x-value maps to exactly one y-value

quiz_questions.explanation: A relation is a function if each input (x-value) maps to exactly one output (y-value). Since no x-value repeats, this is a function.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the set {(2,4), (3,5), (2,6), (4,8)} represent a function?

options.text: Yes, because there are four ordered pairs
No, because x = 2 maps to two different y-values (4 and 6)
No, because 8 is the largest value
Yes, because all y-values are different

quiz_questions.explanation: The x-value 2 appears twice with different y-values (4 and 6), which violates the definition of a function. Each input must have exactly one output.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the vertical line test, a graph represents a function if:

options.text: A vertical line can be drawn that does not touch the graph
A vertical line intersects the graph at more than one point
No vertical line intersects the graph at more than one point
The graph passes through the origin

quiz_questions.explanation: The vertical line test states that a graph represents a function if and only if no vertical line intersects the graph at more than one point. This ensures each x has at most one y.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a relation but NOT a function?
quiz_questions.explanation: {(1,2), (1,3), (2,4)} is a relation but not a function because the input x = 1 has two different outputs (2 and 3). A function requires each input to have exactly one output.

options.text: Not a relation
Not a function because y = 5 repeats
A function only if x-values are positive
A function because each x-value has exactly one y-value

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a function because each x-value (1, 2, 3, 4) maps to exactly one y-value. Multiple x-values can share the same y-value; what matters is that no x-value has multiple y-values.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mapping diagram shows: 1 -> 4, 2 -> 7, 3 -> 4, 4 -> 10. Is this a function?

options.text: No, because 1 and 3 both map to 4
Yes, because each input maps to exactly one output
No, because outputs should not repeat
Yes, but only because inputs don't repeat

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a function because each input (1, 2, 3, 4) maps to exactly one output. It is fine for different inputs to share the same output (both 1 and 3 mapping to 4 is allowed).

quiz_questions.question_text: Does the equation y = x^2 represent a function?

options.text: Yes, because for every x there is exactly one y = x^2
Yes, but only for positive x-values
No, because the graph is curved
No, because x = 2 and x = -2 give the same y-value

quiz_questions.explanation: y = x^2 is a function because each x-value produces exactly one y-value. Although x = 2 and x = -2 both give y = 4, what matters is that each individual x maps to only one y.

quiz_questions.question_text: Does x = y^2 represent y as a function of x?

options.text: Yes, because x and y are related under normal circumstances
Yes, because you can solve for y when evaluated systematically
No, because x is on the wrong side as observed in standard experiments
No, because for most x-values there are two corresponding y-values (positive and negative square roots)

quiz_questions.explanation: For x = y^2, most positive x-values give two y-values (for example, x = 4 gives y = 2 and y = -2). Since one input can produce two outputs, y is not a function of x.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the ordered pair (x, y), x is called the:

options.text: Dependent variable
Output
Input or independent variable
Range value

quiz_questions.explanation: In an ordered pair (x, y), the x-value is the input (also called the independent variable or domain value), and the y-value is the output (also called the dependent variable or range value).

quiz_questions.question_text: A table shows: x = {-2, -1, 0, 1, 2} and y = {4, 1, 0, 1, 4}. Does this table represent a function?

options.text: No, because the y-values are not all unique
No, because y = 1 appears twice
Yes, because each x-value has exactly one y-value
No, because y = 4 appears twice

quiz_questions.explanation: Each x-value (-2, -1, 0, 1, 2) maps to exactly one y-value. Repeated y-values do not disqualify a relation from being a function; only repeated x-values with different y-values would.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a relation?

options.text: A set of ordered pairs that maps each input to exactly one output
An equation that passes the vertical line test
A set where no x-values repeat
Any set of ordered pairs (x, y)

quiz_questions.explanation: A relation is simply any set of ordered pairs. It does not have any restrictions on inputs or outputs. A function is a special type of relation with additional rules.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a function?

options.text: A relation where all outputs are different
A graph that is always a straight line
A relation in which each input (x-value) is paired with exactly one output (y-value)
Any set of ordered pairs

quiz_questions.explanation: A function is a special type of relation where every input (element of the domain) is paired with exactly one output (element of the range). No input can have more than one output.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the domain of a relation?

options.text: The set of all output values
The slope of the graph
The set of all input (x) values
The set of all ordered pairs

quiz_questions.explanation: The domain of a relation is the set of all input values (x-values or first coordinates) in the ordered pairs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the range of a relation?

options.text: The set of all input values
The x-intercept of the graph
The difference between the largest and smallest values
The set of all output (y) values

quiz_questions.explanation: The range of a relation is the set of all output values (y-values or second coordinates) in the ordered pairs.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the vertical line test?

options.text: A test that checks if the graph passes through the y-axis
A test to determine if a graph is a straight line
A visual test where, if any vertical line crosses the graph at more than one point, the graph does not represent a function
A test that measures the slope of the graph

quiz_questions.explanation: The vertical line test determines whether a graph represents a function. If any vertical line can be drawn that intersects the graph at more than one point, the graph is not a function.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a mapping diagram?

options.text: A coordinate plane with plotted points as typically measured in practice
A visual representation that uses arrows to show how each input value is paired with an output value
A table of x and y values, particularly regarding corresponding in most standard conditions
A bar graph showing frequencies in most standard conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: A mapping diagram displays the domain values on one side and range values on the other, with arrows showing how each input maps to its corresponding output.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an input in a function?

options.text: The value substituted into the function, typically the x-value
The result produced by the function
The equation of the function
The name of the function

quiz_questions.explanation: An input is the value from the domain that is substituted into the function. In function notation f(x), the input is x, and the output is f(x).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an output in a function?

options.text: The domain of the function as determined by standard methodology
The x-value placed into the function according to conventional analysis
The slope of the function, particularly regarding when in most standard conditions
The value produced by the function after an input is substituted, typically the y-value

quiz_questions.explanation: An output is the value the function produces when a specific input is substituted. It is the y-value in the ordered pair and represents the result of applying the function rule.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a one-to-one function?

options.text: A function where each output value corresponds to exactly one input value
A function that only has one x-intercept
A function with only one ordered pair
A function where all inputs equal all outputs

quiz_questions.explanation: A one-to-one function (also called an injection) is a function where each output is paired with exactly one input. No two different inputs produce the same output.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean for a relation to 'fail' the vertical line test?

options.text: The graph is not a straight line according to conventional analysis
The graph does not touch the x-axis based on the prevailing evidence
At least one vertical line crosses the graph at two or more points, meaning the relation is not a function
The graph is decreasing, particularly regarding more under normal circumstances

quiz_questions.explanation: Failing the vertical line test means there exists at least one vertical line that intersects the graph in two or more points. This means a single x-value has multiple y-values, so the relation is not a function.

quiz_questions.question_text: A vending machine accepts coins and dispenses one specific snack for each button pressed. Button A gives chips, Button B gives cookies, Button C gives pretzels. Does this vending machine represent a function? What is the domain and range?

options.text: No, because a vending machine is not mathematical
Yes; Domain = {A, B, C}, Range = {chips, cookies, pretzels}
No, because there are only three items
Yes; Domain = {chips, cookies, pretzels}, Range = {A, B, C}

quiz_questions.explanation: The vending machine is a function because each button (input) gives exactly one snack (output). The domain is the set of buttons {A, B, C} and the range is the set of snacks {chips, cookies, pretzels}.

quiz_questions.question_text: A phone plan charges $0.10 per text message. The total monthly text charge y depends on the number of texts x sent. A customer sends 150 texts. What is y, and does this relationship represent a function?

options.text: y = $1.50; yes, it is a function
y = $150; no, because the cost is too high
y = $15; yes, each number of texts produces exactly one charge
y = $15; no, because different people might text different amounts

quiz_questions.explanation: y = 0.10 * 150 = $15. This is a function because each number of texts (x) produces exactly one charge (y). The rule y = 0.10x assigns one output to each input.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher assigns grades based on test scores: 90-100 = A, 80-89 = B, 70-79 = C, 60-69 = D, below 60 = F. A student scores 85. Is the grading system a function from scores to letter grades?

options.text: Yes, but only for passing scores
No, because grades are letters, not numbers
No, because many scores map to the same grade
Yes, because each specific score maps to exactly one letter grade (the student gets a B)

quiz_questions.explanation: Each specific test score maps to exactly one letter grade. A score of 85 gives exactly one grade: B. Multiple scores mapping to the same grade is fine; the key is each score gives only one grade.

quiz_questions.question_text: A social media platform shows that a user can have multiple posts, but each post has exactly one author. Consider the relation from posts to authors. Is this a function?

options.text: No, because posts and authors are not numbers
Yes, but only if the author writes one post
No, because one author can write many posts
Yes, because each post (input) has exactly one author (output)

quiz_questions.explanation: When mapping posts to authors, each post has exactly one author, making this a function. The fact that one author may write multiple posts means it's not one-to-one, but it is still a function.

quiz_questions.question_text: A GPS system maps each address to a set of coordinates (latitude, longitude). However, some buildings have multiple addresses (like a corner store with two street addresses). Does the relation from addresses to coordinates represent a function?

options.text: No, because GPS is not a mathematical function
Yes, but only for residential addresses
No, because multiple addresses can map to the same building
Yes, because each address maps to exactly one set of coordinates

quiz_questions.explanation: Each individual address maps to exactly one location (latitude, longitude), so the relation from addresses to coordinates is a function. Multiple addresses mapping to the same coordinates makes it not one-to-one, but it is still a function.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car rental service charges a flat fee of $25 plus $0.15 per mile driven. After a trip, a customer drove 200 miles. Identify the input, output, and whether this is a function.

options.text: Input: miles driven (200), Output: total cost ($55), Function: yes
Input: 200, Output: 200, Function: no
Input: total cost, Output: miles, Function: no
Input: $25, Output: $0.15, Function: yes

quiz_questions.explanation: Input is miles driven (200). Output: y = 25 + 0.15(200) = 25 + 30 = $55. This is a function because each number of miles driven produces exactly one total cost.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student records the temperature at noon for five days: Monday 72F, Tuesday 68F, Wednesday 75F, Thursday 68F, Friday 80F. Does the relation (day, temperature) represent a function? Does (temperature, day) represent a function?

options.text: Neither is a function, particularly regarding temp under the given assumptions
(day, temp) is a function, but (temp, day) is not because 68F maps to both Tuesday and Thursday
(temp, day) is a function, but (day, temp) is not
Both are functions, particularly regarding records based on the prevailing evidence

quiz_questions.explanation: (day, temp) is a function because each day has exactly one temperature. However, (temp, day) is not a function because 68F maps to two different days (Tuesday and Thursday).

quiz_questions.question_text: A movie streaming service lets users rate movies 1-5 stars. Sarah rates three movies: Movie A = 4 stars, Movie B = 3 stars, Movie C = 4 stars. Does the relation (movie, rating) represent a function? Is it one-to-one?

options.text: It is not a function because two movies have the same rating
It is neither a function nor one-to-one
It is a one-to-one function
It is a function but not one-to-one, since Movie A and C share the same rating

quiz_questions.explanation: Each movie maps to exactly one rating, so it is a function. However, movies A and C both map to 4 stars, so it is not one-to-one (different inputs produce the same output).

quiz_questions.question_text: A barista makes drinks according to the menu: if you order a latte, you always get the same latte recipe. The drink order is the input and the prepared drink is the output. One day, a new employee makes a mocha when a customer orders a latte. Does the barista system still represent a function?

options.text: No, because the input 'latte' now maps to two different outputs (latte and mocha), violating the definition of a function
Yes, because the employee eventually corrected the order
Yes, because drinks are still made
No, because mochas are not on the menu

quiz_questions.explanation: If ordering a latte can result in either a latte or a mocha, then the input 'latte' maps to two different outputs. This violates the definition of a function, where each input must produce exactly one output.

quiz_questions.question_text: A state DMV assigns license plate numbers. Each car gets exactly one plate number, but old numbers can be reassigned to new cars after a car is retired. At any given time, does the relation (car, plate number) represent a function?

options.text: Yes, but only if no plates are reused
No, because numbers get reused
No, because plates are not mathematical
Yes, because at any given time each active car has exactly one plate number

quiz_questions.explanation: At any given time, each car has exactly one license plate, so (car, plate number) is a function. The fact that numbers may be reused over time does not affect this, because at any single point in time, each car still has exactly one plate.
